title: They Are Talking About a Different Reality
pressure: In a team meeting, the manager confidently describes the office as consistent, fair, and well-supported. You hear the words clearly. But your daily experience contradicts nearly every part of it. The assistants have already adapted. Silence has already normalized. The inconsistency is no longer subtle — it is structural. And yet, the person responsible for the system is describing something else entirely.
tradeoff: Maintain alignment with leadership vs trust your own direct observation

cognitive_dynamics.stage_4: Choice: adjust perception vs hold reality

choices.label: Assume the manager has information you don’t
choices.subtext: Defer to authority to reduce internal conflict

choices.label: Recognize clearly that leadership perception is misaligned with reality
choices.subtext: Hold your observation even if it creates tension

choices.label: Disengage from leadership perspective entirely
choices.subtext: Protect your clarity by rejecting their frame

A.escalation_text: Deferring to authority immediately reduces tension. But it requires you to override your own repeated observations. Over time, you begin filtering reality through what leadership says instead of what you see.
identity_clash: Leadership is saying the system works
If I trust them, I have to ignore what I’ve experienced

second_choices.label: Fully adopt leadership perspective
second_choices.cost: You trade lived observation for narrative alignment until inconsistencies feel normal.

second_choices.label: Re-compare reality vs narrative explicitly
second_choices.cost: You must hold tension while comparing claims to what actually happens in the room.

action_decision.prompt: If authority defines truth, what happens to your observation?

choices.label: List specific mismatches between stated standards and actual behavior
Accept the narrative and move on

B.escalation_text: The moment you accept the gap, the problem changes category. This is no longer about fairness mistakes — it is about a system operating on a false self-understanding. That realization increases pressure, not reduces it.
identity_clash: Leadership does not see it the same way
This means the system cannot correct itself from within

second_choices.label: Keep this realization internal
second_choices.cost: The system keeps a false self-understanding while you carry accurate misalignment alone.

second_choices.label: Define the misalignment in concrete structural terms
second_choices.cost: Language that maps the gap may land as accusatory or destabilizing to leadership.

action_decision.prompt: If perception is misaligned, what becomes your reference point?

choices.label: Map exact differences between policy, messaging, and lived behavior

C.escalation_text: Doubting yourself lowers discomfort quickly. But it removes your ability to detect patterns accurately. Over time, your internal compass weakens.
identity_clash: If I doubt myself, I don’t have to resolve this

second_choices.label: Continue self-doubt
second_choices.cost: Self-doubt dulls your mirror; patterns become harder to name with confidence.

second_choices.label: Validate your observation with evidence
second_choices.cost: Evidence commits you; you can no longer retreat to a comfortable maybe.

D.escalation_text: Rejecting leadership protects your perception, but isolates you from influence. The system continues unchanged, just without your engagement.
identity_clash: I don’t want to align with this
If I disengage, I lose any ability to affect change

second_choices.label: Fully disengage
second_choices.cost: You preserve private clarity but forfeit leverage to influence correction from inside.

second_choices.label: Understand how leadership formed their perception
second_choices.cost: Reconstructing their lens costs energy and may not close the underlying reality gap.

action_decision.prompt: Is disconnection a solution or avoidance?

choices.label: Analyze how leadership conclusions were formed
